6. Downstream Predictive-Utility Sensitivity Analysis: Cluster Labels vs MVFS vs Full Baseline Risk Variable Set

Sam A. Sievertsen

  • 6.1 Set Up

  • 6.11 Reproducibility Log

### Dynamic interpretation summary
This sensitivity analysis asks whether the predictive utility in the baseline bipolar-risk architecture is carried mainly by the simple two-cluster label, by the reduced 7-variable MVFS, or only by the full 17-variable set.
For first-onset risk (DTH), the full 17-variable model had the highest McFadden pseudo-R2 for 4 of 4 outcomes, and the MVFS recovered 47.3% of the incremental pseudo-R2 gain achieved by the full model over the cluster model. This indicates that the cluster label retains a meaningful low-dimensional summary of risk, but that some onset-relevant information is lost when the full baseline feature set is collapsed to either the MVFS or the two-cluster label.
For prevalence, the MVFS had the highest AUC for 0 of 4 outcomes, and both the MVFS and full 17-variable predictor blocks were jointly significant for all 4 outcomes. The row-level in-sample AUCs ranged from 0.696 to 0.757 across the competing models. In this context, those AUCs should be interpreted as a descriptive discrimination check on person-wave rows within the analytic sample, rather than as cross-validated subject-level predictive performance.
That distinction is important because the higher-risk cluster still shows large prevalence odds ratios, while the AUC summarizes how well fitted probabilities separate case from non-case rows across the full analytic sample. Together, these results are most useful for comparing relative utility and parsimony across the cluster, MVFS, and full-variable models, rather than for claiming screening-grade prediction.
Taken together, the current preliminary takeaway is that the cluster label remains clinically interpretable and strongly associated with outcome, the MVFS is the most efficient reduced non-cluster representation of the concurrent prevalence signal, and the full 17-variable set retains the strongest utility for first-onset modeling.

6.11 Reproducibility Log
